title: Optimal Exam Review Schedule 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Plan your exam reviews using spaced repetition science. Determine when to study for maximum retention before exam day.
intro: Cramming the night before an exam is the worst possible strategy. Cepeda et al. (2008) research shows that the optimal interval between reviews and your final exam is 10–30% of your total study time. For an exam 30 days away, your final review should be 3–7 days before, not the night before. This calculator distributes 3–5 strategically-spaced reviews across your study timeline, following the forgetting curve to maximize retention on exam day. Perfect for college exams, professional certifications, and standardized tests.
keyTakeaway: For an exam 30 days away, schedule reviews on **day 1, day 7, day 14, and day 25**. Your final review should be **5–7 days before**, never the night before.
useCases: College exam with a confirmed exam date.

example.title: Example: Exam in 30 days, 4 reviews
example.result: 4 reviews scheduled on days 1, 7, 16, and 25. Final review 5 days before your exam.

explanation:
## Principle: Expanding intervals

Each gap between reviews must be longer than the previous one. Using the Anki spacing factor (~2.5):

```
intervals = [1, 3, 7, 15, 35, ...]
```

## For 30 days (4 reviews)

| Review | Day | Interval from previous |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | — |
| 2 | 7 | +6 |
| 3 | 16 | +9 |
| 4 | 25 | +9 |
| Exam | 30 | +5 |

## Final session rule

Your last review must be **5–10% of total study time** before the exam. For a 30-day timeline → 3 days before, never the night before. Late-night cramming destroys sleep, increases anxiety, and ruins retention.

## What to do the night before

- Light review of summary notes (no new material).
- Organize materials and test day logistics.
- Get 8 hours of sleep.
- Eat a balanced meal.

## What NOT to do

- All-night cramming sessions.
- Heavy caffeine late in the day.
- Reviewing material you've never studied.
- Comparing yourself to classmates.

Learn more about the [Ebbinghaus forgetting curve](/calculadora-ebbinghaus-curva-olvido) and [spaced repetition with Anki](/calculadora-repeticion-espaciada-anki-intervalos).

faq.q: How do I use this exam review calculator?
faq.a: Enter the number of days until your exam and select how many review sessions you want. The calculator instantly computes the optimal days for each review based on spacing principles. Everything runs in your browser—no data is sent to any server.

faq.q: Does spaced repetition actually improve exam performance?
faq.a: Yes. Decades of cognitive science research (Cepeda et al., 2008 and others) confirm that spaced review dramatically beats cramming. Distributed practice increases long-term retention and recall under pressure.

faq.q: What if I only have 3–5 days before my exam?
faq.a: You can still benefit. Use 3 reviews with shorter intervals (days 1, 2, 3) and focus heavily on active recall. Prioritize the most important concepts rather than trying to cover everything.

faq.q: What if I miss a scheduled review day?
faq.a: Don't panic. Do that review the next day and adjust the remaining schedule accordingly. A delayed review is far better than skipping it entirely. You can shift the final review by one day if needed.

faq.q: Should I use active recall or just re-read my notes?
faq.a: Active recall is dramatically more effective—studies show 70%+ better retention compared to passive re-reading. Use flashcards, self-testing, or the Feynman Technique rather than just reviewing notes.

faq.q: Do practice exams and mock tests count as review sessions?
faq.a: Absolutely—they're among the most powerful review tools available. Plan 1–2 full practice tests in the final 5–7 days before your exam. They combine retrieval practice with real exam conditions and confidence building.

It's based on peer-reviewed research cited in the Sources section and serves as a guideline. Results are indicative. For critical decisions (medical, legal, major financial), always consult a qualified professional.

faq.q: Is this calculator free and do I need to sign up?
faq.a: Completely free—no email, registration, or personal data required. Use it as many times as you want.
